title: Antiderivatives and indefinite integrals
description: Test your skills at finding antiderivatives.
topic_path: The fundamental theorem of calculus and definite integrals

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[x^3-4x^2+C\right]&=3x^2-8x
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[2x^2+5x+C\right]&=4x+5
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[3x^3-7x+C\right]&=9x^2-7
\end{align}$

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Integral | Result
:- | :-
$\displaystyle\int (4x+5)\,dx$ | $2x^2+5x+C$
$\displaystyle\int (3x^2-8x)\,dx$ | $x^3-4x^2+C$
$\displaystyle\int (9x^2-7)\,dx$ | $3x^3-7x+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $y>0$ and $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Integral | Result
:- | :-
$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{12y^2}{4y^3+1}\,dy$ | $\ln (20y^3+5)+C$
$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{20y^3}{5y^4+1}\,dy$ | $\ln (5y^4+1)+C$
$\displaystyle\int \dfrac{5y^4+1}{y^5+y}\,dy$ | $\ln(y^5+y )+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[7\sin(8z-5)+C\right]&=56\cos(8z-5)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[8\cos(7z-5)+C\right]&=-56\sin(7z-5)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[8\sin(7z-5)+C\right]&=56\cos(7z-5)
\end{align}$

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Integral | Result
:- | :-
$\displaystyle\int 56\cos(8z-5)\,dz$ | $7\sin(8z-5)+C$
$\displaystyle\int -56\sin(7z-5)\,dz$ | $8\cos(7z-5)+C$
$\displaystyle\int 56\cos(7z-5)\,dz$ | $8\sin(7z-5)+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[t^4-4t^2+C\right]&=4t^3-8t
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[8t^3+8t^2+C\right]&=24t^2+16t
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[6t^2+12t+C\right]&=12t+12
\end{align}$

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Result | Integral
:- | :-
$t^4-4t^2+C$ | $\displaystyle\int 4t^3-8t\,dt$
$8t^3+8t^2+C$ | $\displaystyle\int 24t^2+16t\,dt$
$6t^2+12t+C$ | $\displaystyle\int 12t+12\,dt$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $x>2$ and $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Result | Integral
:- | :-
$\ln(4x-8)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x-2}\,dx$
$\ln(4x^4-8)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{4x^3}{x^4-2}\,dx$
$\ln(4x)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int \dfrac{1}{x}\,dx$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each indefinite integral to its result, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an *antiderivative* of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

The *indefinite integral* of a function is the family of all antiderivatives of that function. In other words, $\displaystyle\int f(x)\,dx$ is an expression of the form $F(x)+C$ where $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ and $C$ represents any constant.

[[â˜ƒ explanation 1]]

Let's differentiate the functions on the **Result** column and match them to their indefinite integrals.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[-8\cos(5y+4)+C\right]&=40\sin(5y+4)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[8\cos(5y+1)+C\right]&=-40\sin(5y+1)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[-8\sin(5y+4)+C\right]&=-40\cos(5y+4)
\end{align}$

**In conclusion, these are the equivalent matches.**

Result | Integral
:- | :-
$-8\cos(5y+4)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int 40\sin(5y+4)\,dy$
$8\cos(5y+1)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int -40\sin(5y+1)\,dy$
$-8\sin(5y+4)+C$ | $\displaystyle\int -40\cos(5y+4)\,dy$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\sin(2x-5)+C\right]&=2\cos(2x-5)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[\cos(2x+5)+C\right]&=-2\sin(2x+5)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dx}\left[2\sin(x-5)+C\right]&=2\cos(x-5)
\end{align}$

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Function| Antiderivative
:- | :-
$f(x)=2\cos(2x-5)$| $F(x)=\sin(2x-5)+C$
$f(x)=-2\sin(2x+5)$| $F(x)=\cos(2x+5)+C$
$f(x)=2\cos(x-5)$| $F(x)=2\sin(x-5)+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[2y-8y^4+C\right]&=2-32y^3
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[y^2+2y+C\right]&=2y+2
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dy}\left[y^3+16y^2+C\right]&=3y^2+32y
\end{align}$

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Function| Antiderivative
:- | :-
$g(y)=2-32y^3$| $G(y)=2y-8y^4+C$
$g(y)=2y+2$| $G(y)=y^2+2y+C$
$g(y)=3y^2+32y$| $G(y)=y^3+16y^2+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $z>0$ and $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Function| Antiderivative
:- | :-
$h(z)=\dfrac{8z^2+2}{2z^3+z}$| $H(z)=\ln(6z^4+3z^2)+C$
$h(z)=\dfrac{4z+1}{2z^2+z}$| $H(z)=\ln(6z^2+3z)+C$
$h(z)=\dfrac{4}{4z+1}$| $H(z)=\ln(4z+1)+C$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[4\cos(-2t+7)+C\right]&=8\sin(-2t+7)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[5\sin(-2t+7)+C\right]&=-10\cos(-2t+7)
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dt}\left[\cos(2t+7)+C\right]&=-2\sin(2t+7)
\end{align}$

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Antiderivative | Function
:- | :-
$F(t)=4\cos(-2t+7)+C$ | $f(t)=8\sin(-2t+7)$
$F(t)=5\sin(-2t+7)+C$ | $f(t)=-10\cos(-2t+7)$
$F(t)=\cos(2t+7)+C$ | $f(t)=-2\sin(2t+7)$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[2z^3+5z+C\right]&=6z^2+5
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[z^3+5z+C\right]&=3z^2+5
\\\\
\dfrac{d}{dz}\left[6z^3+5z^2+C\right]&=18z^2+10z
\end{align}$

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Antiderivative | Function
:- | :-
$G(z)=2z^3+5z+C$ | $g(z)=6z^2+5$
$G(z)=z^3+5z+C$ | $g(z)=3z^2+5$
$G(z)=6z^3+5z^2+C$ | $g(z)=18z^2+10z$

assessment_items.question_content:
**Match each function to its antiderivative, where $t>3$ and $C$ is a constant.**

[[â˜ƒ matcher 1]]
hints:
A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$ if:

$F'(x)=f(x)$

for all $x$ in the domain of $f$.

Let's differentiate the antiderivatives and match them to their derivative functions.

In conclusion, these are the matches between the functions and antiderivatives.

Antiderivative | Function
:- | :-
$H(t)=\ln(12t^2-12 )+C$ | $h(t)=\dfrac{2t}{t^2-1}$
$H(t)=\ln (t^4-6t^2) +C$ | $h(t)=\dfrac{4t^2-12}{t^3-6t}$
$H(t)=\ln (4t^3-12t)+C$ | $h(t)=\dfrac{3t^2-3}{t^3-3t}$
